corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: We introduce a class of CNNs called deep convolutional generative adversarial networks (DCGANs), that have certain architectural constraints, and demonstrate that they are a strong candidate for unsupervised learning.

context_window.previous: In this work we hope to help bridge the gap between the success of CNNs for supervised learning and unsuper- vised learning.
context_window.current: We introduce a class of CNNs called deep convolutional generative adversarial networks (DCGANs), that have certain architectural constraints, and demonstrate that they are a strong candidate for unsupervised learning.
context_window.next: Training on various image datasets, we show convincing evidence that our deep convolu- tional adversarial pair learns a hierarchy of representations from object parts to scenes in both the generator and discriminator.

location.sentence: Architecture guidelines for stable Deep Convolutional GANs • Replace any pooling layers with strided convolutions (discriminator) and fractional-strided convolutions (generator). • Use batchnorm in both the generator and the discriminator. • Remove fully connected hidden layers for deeper architectures. • Use ReLU activation in generator for all layers except for the output, which uses Tanh. • Use LeakyReLU activation in the discriminator for all layers.

context_window.previous: This is in contrast to the original GAN paper, which used the maxout activation (Goodfellow et al., 2013).
context_window.current: Architecture guidelines for stable Deep Convolutional GANs • Replace any pooling layers with strided convolutions (discriminator) and fractional-strided convolutions (generator). • Use batchnorm in both the generator and the discriminator. • Remove fully connected hidden layers for deeper architectures. • Use ReLU activation in generator for all layers except for the output, which uses Tanh. • Use LeakyReLU activation in the discriminator for all layers.
context_window.next: We trained DCGANs on three datasets, Large-scale Scene Understanding (LSUN) (Yu et al., 2015), Imagenet-1k and a newly assembled Faces dataset.

top_candidates.sentence: Architecture guidelines for stable Deep Convolutional GANs • Replace any pooling layers with strided convolutions (discriminator) and fractional-strided convolutions (generator). • Use batchnorm in both the generator and the discriminator. • Remove fully connected hidden layers for deeper architectures. • Use ReLU activation in generator for all layers except for the output, which uses Tanh. • Use LeakyReLU activation in the discriminator for all layers.
See Fig. for a visualization of an example model architecture.

We introduce a class of CNNs called deep convolutional generative adversarial networks (DCGANs), that have certain architectural constraints, and demonstrate that they are a strong candidate for unsupervised learning.

We propose that one way to build good image representations is by training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) , and later reusing parts of the generator and discriminator networks as feature extractors for supervised tasks.
In this paper, we make the following contributions • We propose and evaluate a set of constraints on the architectural topology of Convolutional GANs that make them stable to train in most settings.
The strongest example of this is global average pooling which has been utilized in state of the art image classification models .

We propose a more stable set of architectures for training generative adversarial networks and we give evidence that adversarial networks learn good representations of images for supervised learning and generative modeling.
We think that extending this framework to other domains such as video (for frame prediction) and audio (pre-trained features for speech synthesis) should be very interesting.

downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
